skill.name: changelog-generator
skill.description: Generate structured changelogs from git commit history using Conventional Commits, semantic versioning, and Keep a Changelog conventions.
skill.summary: Create release notes and changelog entries from git commits.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, and environment-token patterns in SKILL.md. Review shows these are mainly documentation snippets for changelog generation, but one Node.js example constructs shell commands from a tag option before calling execSync. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, or active exfiltration in the skill file.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Shell Command Construction in Example Script
medium_findings.description: The Node.js example builds git command strings and passes them to execSync. The tag value can come from a command-line option, so copying this sample without validation can create command injection risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is in a documentation code block, so it is not active malware. The command string is still built from an option value and executed through a shell API, which is a real copy-paste security risk.

low_findings.title: Release Workflow Examples Reference Repository Tokens
low_findings.description: The GitHub Actions examples reference NPM_TOKEN and GITHUB_TOKEN. This is standard release automation, but users should restrict token scope and avoid exposing generated release output from untrusted commits.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token references are visible in CI examples and are legitimate for release workflows. The risk is contextual because copied workflows can run with repository secrets.

low_findings.title: Expected Local Changelog File Writes
low_findings.description: The example scripts read and write CHANGELOG.md in the current repository. This is expected behavior for this skill, but users should review generated content before committing it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem activity is explicitly limited to CHANGELOG.md in sample code. This is normal for a changelog tool and does not show broader file access.

low_findings.title: Static Path Traversal and Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal hits are markdown links to relative commit pages, and weak-crypto alerts appear to match words such as hash in changelog examples. No evidence found of path traversal, cryptographic misuse, or obfuscated active code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are markdown link construction and commit hash formatting. I did not find evidence that they access parent directories or implement cryptography.

dangerous_patterns.title: Synchronous Shell Execution in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes execSync examples for git commands. This is functional for changelog generation, but copied code should validate tag names or use argument arrays.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous API usage is clearly present, but it appears in educational sample code rather than an executable skill payload.

dangerous_patterns.title: CI Release Workflow Uses Repository Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: The release examples include package publishing and GitHub release creation with secrets. This is expected release automation, but it expands risk if used on unreviewed repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow snippets reference real secret variables but do not exfiltrate them. Risk depends on how users copy and authorize the workflow.

content.value_statement: Release notes are hard to keep consistent when commit history grows. This skill turns git commits into organized changelog sections with version guidance.
actual_capabilities: Groups Conventional Commit messages into changelog sections.
Highlights breaking changes and suggests semantic version bumps.
Formats output using Keep a Changelog style.
Creates release-note text for unreleased changes or tagged versions.
Includes examples for shell, Node.js, Python, and GitHub Actions workflows.
limitations: Works best when commit messages follow Conventional Commits.
Generated changelogs still need human review before release.
Example scripts may require security hardening before production use.
Does not verify issue links, pull request links, or release metadata automatically.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Library Release
use_cases.description: Summarize new features, fixes, and breaking changes before publishing a package version.

use_cases.title: Draft Product Release Notes
use_cases.description: Convert engineering commits into clear release notes for customers and internal teams.

use_cases.title: Standardize Release Automation
use_cases.description: Create consistent changelog templates and workflow examples for repositories using semantic versioning.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Changelog
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a changelog for the current repository from the latest tag to HEAD. Group entries by Added, Changed, Fixed, and Security.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Unreleased Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze commits since the last release and draft an Unreleased section. Call out breaking changes and unclear commit messages.

prompt_templates.title: Recommend a Version Bump
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the commit history since v1.4.0, suggest the next semantic version, and explain the reason for the bump.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Release Note Draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate customer-facing release notes from Conventional Commits, include migration notes for breaking changes, and mark entries that need manual verification.

output_examples.input: Commits include feat: add export flow, fix: repair pagination, and docs: update setup guide.
output_examples.output: The changelog contains Added, Fixed, and Documentation sections with concise entries for each commit.

output_examples.input: A commit uses feat!: replace authentication provider and includes a breaking change note.
output_examples.output: The release draft places the authentication change in a breaking changes section and recommends a major version bump.

output_examples.input: A repository has many chore and test commits before a patch release.
output_examples.output: The output keeps user-facing fixes prominent and moves internal maintenance into a separate low-priority section.

best_practices: Use Conventional Commits before relying on automated grouping.
Review generated entries for customer impact and accuracy.
Validate release scripts before running them with repository secrets.
anti_patterns: Do not publish raw commit logs as customer release notes.
Do not hide breaking changes inside general change sections.
Do not run copied shell examples with untrusted tag names.

faq.question: Does this skill require Conventional Commits?
faq.answer: No, but Conventional Commits produce the most accurate grouping and version recommendations.

faq.question: Can it suggest semantic versions?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses breaking changes, features, and fixes to suggest major, minor, or patch releases.

faq.question: Can it update an existing CHANGELOG.md?
faq.answer: The examples show how to insert new entries, but the final file should be reviewed before committing.

faq.question: Does it create GitHub releases?
faq.answer: It includes GitHub Actions examples for release notes, but it does not run release workflows by itself.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with private repositories?
faq.answer: Yes, if commit data is handled locally and release tokens are scoped to the minimum required permissions.

faq.question: What should I check before publishing output?
faq.answer: Check breaking changes, customer wording, issue links, version numbers, and any generated automation scripts.
